speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number three with Jack Rosenthal. Jack, were you married at this time, in 1938?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about--

speaker: interviewee
text: November 15, 1938, I was married.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife was Celine Rosenthal. She was born a [INAUDIBLE]. And also, by coincidence, I met my wife August 7, 1937. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In 1937, from January to May, I worked the stall, the pavilion of cinema to the World's Fair in Paris. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In May my job was finished, and I left for Egypt. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was [INAUDIBLE]. And then from Egypt, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I came back August 7, 1937 to Paris. And I was coming into my-- I was already in the building, but I was living on third floor. And my concierge stopped to tell me that there's a lady's coming here every day. Didn't speak a word of French, but she had a letter with my address. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he said he couldn't understand what she wants. She said, I'll come tomorrow, [FRENCH]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that was-- she was in Paris, August 7 she was in Paris for 14 days, and went every day to same place. And the concierge said, [FRENCH]. And I came up from Egypt, and in just Egypt it was very hot. And I came-- I had shorts, colonial shorts, and all kind of things. And us-- the concierge takes up my baggage, luggage, and he wants to put the-- put in the key. And he said, [FRENCH]. [LAUGHS] She came the same. And I said, come in, so she came into the house. I said let-- she asked me if I speak Polish. I said yes. And she gives me a letter of my best friend from Warsaw. And he was in that time, was one of first-- he was graduated sports. And he was a judge to boxing.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the letter was-- I didn't know even about it. So she gives me the letter, and I, I stay here with my luggage, I said, give him a chance. I said go in the living room, sit down. And I changed myself. And she said she's leaving tomorrow. I said why do you leaving tomorrow? She says she has a visa only for 15 days, and she came specifically to see this show. [INAUDIBLE] show 1937 in Paris. So I, without knowing it, I said, did you go to the show? And she said, no. [LAUGHS] She doesn't speak any French. She doesn't know anybody. [LAUGHS] So she's ready to go back home. So that's how I met my wife. So she stayed then with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah?

speaker: interviewer
text: She stayed.

speaker: interviewee
text: She-- no, she was living, she had an address she was staying Jewish family from Romania. She had some connections.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I mean she stayed in Paris. She stayed--

speaker: interviewee
text: She remained in Paris--

speaker: interviewer
text: --and you got married.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah, but, so two or three weeks later, I met some people that had a prolongation for a stay permit, to stay for another six month. And I left in November. I left her in my house. And I had to go back-- I had to go to Baghdad.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in 1937, [LAUGHS] to called from Paris to Baghdad [LAUGHS] was like today, it's easier to call from here to China. And she called, every day she called me.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I came back, and six months later we were married. A Jewish marriage, she didn't have permit to be in France. So the  French doesn't recognize this. So we couldn't get married legally. So we were married by rabbi. It was a wedding in the rabbi's house. Now you also had a young son, when, when did he-- when did he show up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Claude showed up the 27th of May, 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so that was later.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not later. 1939. '39. Claude was born July 27, 1929-- 1939. Six weeks before the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Tell me about what was going on now, when the war broke out for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, the war broke out. I had to join my regiment four days after the war broke out. I went to, to my regiment, and, and two weeks later the-- it quieted down. The shock was over. Go home. I went home till February. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] February I was called back to the, to the army. And they assigned me to the Polish, new Polish army. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] In, in that time, and this is late February, 1940. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In the end of May, my regiment received an order. They have to move. So there was a train waiting for us, they put us in a train. And for two or three days, from close to the border of Germany, close to the Maginot line. It was two or three days till we came to a place we didn't know what it is, or where it is even. And directly from the train, aboard ship. And the ship was already backed, already, I think. And we went way. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Eight days later, I woke up in the morning, and I find myself in the water. And in the water was like a fire, all around. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: That's all that I remember. And then I woke up, when I find myself aboard the ship. I said, where they going? I didn't know where I am, even. He said they going to London, to England.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said where was, where was I? They told me the name is the Bay of Narvik.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that I was torpedoed. And we came back to France, we came back further than directly to London. From Narvik, London is first. And from there, from London, they took us back-- they took off everybody that was British in London. And anybody who was not British. There were Czechs, and there were Polocks also. There were French. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I came back to France, it was 11th of June, 1940. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And that was-- the place that we left, that was Dunkirk. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So they gave us an assign-- they gave me an assignment [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to be go to join my regiment and the, the two guys that I had with me all the time. [INAUDIBLE] just said, let's go home, let's go home. I said wait, we just came back from a big trip, victoriously. [LAUGHS] So I came-- where I came, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was too late. Four hours before, they moved out, till the 16th of June, 1940. I had an assignment to go hold a position. And the position was an anti-air gun. But the guy that I took over the position, he said, never shoot. So I said, so why are we standing here, why do I have-- he said, don't worry. He said, if you're going to shoot, they'll kill you. I said who will kill? He said, it's an old gun. [LAUGHS] It will shoot you back. So in the same-- the same day, the 16th of June, they came down the Messerschmidt. The Germans-- the Germans had already occupied Paris. Paris was occupied June the 14th.

speaker: interviewee
text: They came down in the [INAUDIBLE] machine guns and message. So I was wounded, and I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was June the 17th. Early in the morning, I only remember one thing [INAUDIBLE] holding the position. We were sleeping on our bellies, and I said to it-- with my arms I made like this. I said, it's raining. And I woke up. When I woke up, I saw the sun. There was standing a guy, who had the Red Cross. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said to, to me-- he said to me, he said, what are you doing here? I only remember, I said, save me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Later on, I realized it was a student, a Romanian student. There was a medical school, and he, he was-- he became a volunteer. He volunteered to the army. And that was the guy. I never saw the guy. I never heard any trace from the guy anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was brought into the hospital. And the hospital, the first thing there was on stretchers, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When I saw the sun, must have been maybe 6:00 in the morning, or 5:00 in the morning. It was summer already. And so the three stretchers. I am [INAUDIBLE] two guys on the stretcher bed. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So the first thing, they didn't look even what I have anything. They give an injection, a tetanus injection. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in the same hospital in the morning-- this was already the 17th in the morning-- I came in the morning, very early in the morning, they said, anybody that can walk should walk out. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there was a list of 10 or 15 names they called. And for the 10 or 15 names, there was an ambulance would take you. I was between the 10 and 15 names, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so I couldn't walk at that time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I came into the ambulance and there were maybe 8 to 10 was already in the ambulance on stretchers. And they start to say that they lost the war, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and the fault is for Mr Rosenfelt. He promised us to help us. They couldn't say Roosevelt. Mr. Rosenfelt, he says the, the dirty Jews. They lost the war only. So I called the chauffeur for the ambulance. I said, I must get out, I cannot stay. I didn't say what. So he's giving me two, two canes. I was walking with two canes. Walked out, maybe, maybe I made 10 meters. And he said, the Germans are going to occupy it. Walk, walk towards Bordeaux. And what was the name of the city you were in at this point, where the hospital was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where the hospital. Pithiviers, that was about 25 kilometers away, on the way to Angouleme. Angouleme in Bordeaux. Next.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what--

speaker: interviewee
text: Pithiviers, that was--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of injury did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had my right arm, and I had badly-- felt very badly in the back. I didn't know what I had. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I walked out, and I saw a guy with a blindfold on one eye. And I said to the guy, where you going? He said, I don't see anything. I tell him, I said, you know, I have a hard time to walk, let's go together.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the guy's name was [INAUDIBLE], he told me, his name was [INAUDIBLE]. And he was an electrician in Paris, he said. So we went a few, maybe, maybe 500 or 300, I don't know, maybe less. And they were all on the highways. All, all cars, abundant cars, abundant people. All cars. Couldn't even, even walking. You couldn't walk.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said to the guy, you have a knife? He said, yes, I have a little knife. So I looked for a car that has-- without the roof, you know, that you can-- [FRENCH], that you can take off. And there was a little car, a small, little car.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I touched the roof, and the roof fell down. [LAUGHS] So I said I don't need your knife. I said, hold me, so I can look see if there's gas, gasoline. So I called, didn't have any gasoline. There was a little bit of gasoline in the car. So we moved in in the car, and I was driving the car. This was already after Angouleme, towards Bordeaux. We came into the [INAUDIBLE] to Bordeaux. The first thing we ask for is the hospital. So they told us, over there, go to the hospital. We went to the hospital, and Bordeaux was bombarded the same night. And they had 150 killed at that time. So no place for anybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they give us an injection for me, an injection for the guy. And they tell us to go to the station, railroad station. So I said, well, what is this station? There's a hospital. He said there's a military train with the hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So we came-- super, we're going out in Bordeaux, from the hospital, they stole our car. [LAUGHS] So in every comedy, there's tragedy. So what happened? You went to the military train at the station?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the military train in the-- and they took us right away to the hospital, to the hospital part of the train. And we came in to Plou. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in Plou, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there's a lady approached me. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I didn't see the lady maybe for four, five years. I knew the lady in Paris. She was active also in, in American colonies, this lady.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said, you don't remember me? I said no. [LAUGHS] She was the head volunteer for the Red Cross. And she said, look, go in in this train, and the train will take you to the border. I said, why can't we Stay here She said the Germans are going to occupy Plou. Now, all this time, what was going-- where was your wife and son?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I left them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you hadn't had any contact with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: No contact since, since February 1940. But in February, I was there for 24 hours. And she told me that she had a help for the child. That she'll go-- in case something happen in Paris, she'll go to the parents of this girl. And she mentioned to me a name, Prat. So that was the last time before the war I saw my wife. And then when I was in-- brought from Poul to Bayonne on the border. What is only-- the border is [INAUDIBLE] it's called. It's about two kilometers, and it was a Catholic old hospitals and the Catholic sisters.

speaker: interviewee
text: So at night, one sister came to me and she said, do you want me to write a letter to your family? I said yes. And I, I said, we can only write a few words. Tell her I'm all right, and what's happened to the family. And I give her my address, she said no. I said, why no? She said, it's occupied by the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So she asked me, I know, did you have any other family. I said, yes. I didn't realize that she told me it was occupied by the Germans. Gave her another name. And this is only 20 kilometers away from Paris. No. And she kind of was annoyed. Without even thinking, I said, can you write to America? She said she doesn't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I said I have a good friend in America. She said, give me the name and I'll see. So I give her the name, a short name. J-O-S Fox. And he was living in that time in the, in the same building that Mrs. Roosevelt was living, 30 Fifth Avenue. That was a short address. And that was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] five or six days later, I left the hospital. It was already-- armistice was already signed. I think the armistice was signed the 24th of June, 1940. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I find myself what was called Liberzone, the unoccupied zone of France. But this is on the borders, so the border was occupied by Germans, by Nazis. And I say where can I go? He said, go to Toulouse.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So, also, this lady from Plou, she arranged the military hospital. I went to Toulouse, and Toulouse. Too many people in Toulouse. The train will go to Perpignan. I said, why Perpignan? There's a big hospital in Perpignan. So in the same train, three or four days later, in the train I asked the conductor. All the help, all the help was Swiss, but the conductor was a Frenchman. The Swiss supposed to be neutral. So the help in the train was Swiss. I said to the conductor, you heard of a town of Prat? Oh, he said, yes, there's a town there up in the hills, in the mountains, high up. I said, where is it? He said after Perpignan. So I said to him, will you let me out. He said I don't [INAUDIBLE]. So I walked maybe two kilometers from the train. And I came to the station, maybe 6:00 or 7:00 in the morning. Thousands of people, well. When I go to get a ticket, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and she said, yes, there's one train a day.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You don't need a ticket, she said. So I went to Prat. And this is over 60 kilo-- less than 60, it was about 35, 40 kilometers away from Perpignan. And in the other side of the mountains is the [INAUDIBLE], the Republic of Andorra, [FRENCH]. And the, the lady that told me you don't need a ticket said, and the same train goes back in evening.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to Prat. And Prat was a village in that time for maybe 1,200 to 1,500 people. But as all the refugees and all the people, there was maybe 10,000 people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Finally, with difficulty, I come into the [INAUDIBLE], the City Hall. And don't look for anybody else, I wanted to talk to the mayor. I'm a big hero. I'll talk to the mayor. Came in to the mayor, and he said what can do for me. I said, I'm looking for my family. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, what's your name? I said, Rosenthal, I said. He looked at me, and said, what's your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Rosenthal. And he asked me, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you have a son? I said yes. And he tells me, so this son's name is Claude? I said yes, Claude Rosenthal. He said, don't worry. Your wife and child is in my house.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're going to go on to another tape.
